summary:
The Fourth Crusade (1202–1204 AD) was one of the most consequential and controversial of the [[event:92|Crusades]], famous for never reaching the Holy Land but instead sacking Constantinople — the greatest Christian city in the world — and establishing a Latin Empire in its place, an act of shocking betrayal that permanently poisoned relations between the Eastern Orthodox and Roman Catholic churches. The crusade was originally organized by Pope Innocent III to attack Egypt, but financial difficulties led the crusaders to divert first to the Christian city of Zara (on behalf of Venice), and then to Constantinople, where they became entangled in Byzantine dynastic politics.

The sack of Constantinople (April 1204) was one of the worst acts of urban violence in medieval history. The city that had withstood [[event:2203|Arab sieges]], [[event:2954|Umayyad naval assaults]], and Bulgarian invasions for seven centuries was looted by fellow Christians. Libraries, churches, and palaces accumulated over a millennium were stripped; the Hagia Sophia was desecrated; the famous bronze horses of the Hippodrome (still visible at St. Mark's Basilica in Venice today) were stolen along with thousands of other treasures. The Latin Empire established in Constantinople survived until 1261, when the Byzantine emperor Michael VIII Palaiologos recaptured the city — but the restored Byzantine Empire was permanently weakened, economically crippled, and territorially shrunken, leaving it vulnerable to Ottoman expansion and unable to defend itself without Western help. The Fourth Crusade's betrayal of Constantinople is still cited as a major cause of the Great Schism's irreversibility.
